summary:
When preparing proximal walls for Class II cavities in deciduous molars, it is essential to follow the diagonal arrangement of enamel rods running from the dentino-enamel junction towards the occlusal surface; overly invasive procedures can lead to weakened structures due to immature dental tissues and increased risk of attrition as they develop further. To ensure optimal outcomes, practitioners should adopt careful, conservative approaches focusing on minimal tissue removal and maintaining structural integrity throughout treatment planning stages leading up until final restorations are placed securely within prepared sites ensuring longevity post-procedure through meticulous attention detail execution precision skillful craftsmanship informed  Agent2's reasoning (Round 4):
The primary consideration when treating such areas involves understanding the specific anatomy where enamel rods converge at varying angles along their path down into each cusp area. This anatomical feature necessitates caution against unnecessary drilling which may disrupt vital parts of the remaining healthy tooth structure necessary for proper function and protection later in child’s developmental timeline. Additionally, preserving more sound material helps maintain strength needed especially considering young individuals’ active eating habits likely involving harder foods potentially causing additional
